coredata.dc:description:
The antibiotic tetracycline (TC) significantly pollutes water bodies, adversely impacting ecosystems and human health. In this work, a bifunctional platform for simultaneous detection and removal of TC was successfully constructed by in-situ growth of Zr-MOF in BC microspheres. The in-situ growth ensured the stability, while the design of the aerogel microspheres improved the processability, convenience, and recyclability. The macropores and mesopores in the aerogel microspheres significantly improved the molecular mass transfer efficiency, and the sensitivity and selectivity of TC detection and adsorption were improved due to the size-sieving effect of the abundant micropores of Zr-MOF and the supramolecular interaction of the ligand. Owing to the hierarchical pore structure, the adsorption capacity reaches as high as 317.6 mg/g. The enrichment during the adsorption process enhances the interaction between TC and Zr-MOF, thereby significantly improving the detection sensitivity of TC. As expected, BMAT3H5 has a LOD as low as 28 ± 0.012 nM and a K
SV as high as 1.89 ± 0.001 × 106 M−1, providing excellent detection performance compared to other work in recent years. The good selectivity to TC was theoretically validated through simulations with Materials Studio software (MS). It provides a novel and practical bifunctional platform for efficient fluorescence detection and adsorption of TC, which has a broad application prospect in the fields of environmental monitoring, water treatment, and food safety testing.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The antibiotic tetracycline (TC) significantly pollutes water bodies, adversely impacting ecosystems and human health. In this work, a bifunctional platform for simultaneous detection and removal of TC was successfully constructed by in-situ growth of Zr-MOF in BC microspheres. The in-situ growth ensured the stability, while the design of the aerogel microspheres improved the processability, convenience, and recyclability. The macropores and mesopores in the aerogel microspheres significantly improved the molecular mass transfer efficiency, and the sensitivity and selectivity of TC detection and adsorption were improved due to the size-sieving effect of the abundant micropores of Zr-MOF and the supramolecular interaction of the ligand. Owing to the hierarchical pore structure, the adsorption capacity reaches as high as 317.6 mg/g. The enrichment during the adsorption process enhances the interaction between TC and Zr-MOF, thereby significantly improving the detection sensitivity of TC. As expected, BMAT3H5 has a LOD as low as 28 ± 0.012 nM and a K
SV as high as 1.89 ± 0.001 × 106 M−1, providing excellent detection performance compared to other work in recent years. The good selectivity to TC was theoretically validated through simulations with Materials Studio software (MS). It provides a novel and practical bifunctional platform for efficient fluorescence detection and adsorption of TC, which has a broad application prospect in the fields of environmental monitoring, water treatment, and food safety testing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC), a prevalent broad-spectrum antibiotic, is extensively utilized in aquaculture and animal husbandry due to its low cost, significant effect, low toxicity, and broad antimicrobial coverage (Wang et al., 2024). However, TC's resistance to natural degradation leads to significant environmental and food safety concerns, posing a potential hazard to human health (Wu et al., 2024). Consequently, the efficient detection and removal of residual TC in water are paramount for ecological sustainability and human well-being. To date, various techniques have been employed for TC detection, including liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Gab-Allah et al., 2023), enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) (Gao et al., 2019), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Khan et al., 2022), and fluorescence (S. Q. Zhang et al., 2022). However, these methods have limitations, such as the high cost and time-consuming nature of LC-MS, and the intricate sample preparation required for ELISA (Li et al., 2023). In contrast, the fluorescence method presents remarkable advantages due to its simplicity, rapid response, and high sensitivity. Numerous fluorescent materials have been introduced for TC detection, including quantum dots (J. H. Yu et al., 2021), nanoclusters (H. B. Wang, Tao, Mao, Xiao, & Liu, 2021), molecularly imprinted polymers (Wang et al., 2022), and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Gan et al., 2021).

MOFs, porous materials constructed from organic ligands and metal ions (Kalaj et al., 2020), stand out for their high crystallinity, vast specific surface area, controllable pore structure, distinctive physicochemical properties, and adaptable host-guest interactions (Kumar et al., 2022). MOFs have garnered widespread attention across diverse fields, notably in fluorescence sensing (Jia, He, & Wang, 2023), gas adsorption(Q. Zhang, Yan, Yan, & Lv, 2023), controlled drug release (Orellana-Tavra et al., 2015), energy storage (Duan et al., 2023), and catalysis (Wang et al., 2024). Especially, MOFs as fluorescent probes have unique detection advantages for target objects, such as good selectivity, high sensitivity, low detection limit, fast response, and real-time monitoring (Cheng, Hu, Zhou, Wang, & Zhang, 2016). Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2024) developed a boron difluoride-modified Zn-MOF3 with a remarkable TC detection limit of 28.4 nM. Moreover, leveraging their high surface area, MOFs are efficient TC adsorbents (Abazari, Mahjoub, & Shariati, 2019). Yu et al. synthesized MIL-68(Al)/GO composites using sodium alginate, achieving a TC adsorption capacity of 228 mg/g in aqueous solutions (L. L. Yu, Cao, Wu, Yang, & Cheng, 2018). Among many MOFs, Zr-based MOFs have attracted much attention due to their multiple advantages, such as excellent chemical stability, tunable pore size and functionalization, high thermal stability, and abundant synthesis strategies (Bai et al., 2016). While simultaneous TC detection and removal are vital for environmental ease, MOFs' crystalline powder form hinders processing, application, and recycling. To overcome these, blending MOF particles with polymers is emerging as a promising solution (H. P. Wang, Qian, & An, 2022). This hybrid approach offers pore refinement, stability boost, better processability, and enhanced reactivity in composites, thereby advancing their environmental applications.(Hammi et al., 2022).

Nanocellulose (NC), an abundant and eco-friendly biopolymer, boasts low cost, tunable surface chemistry, biodegradability, excellent processability, and formability (Yan et al., 2024). NC-based porous aerogels, renowned for their high porosity, vast specific surface area, and superior adsorption capacity (Shahzad et al., 2023). Incorporating MOFs via in-situ growth on NC aerogels innovates TC detection and removal (Xia et al., 2023). Elevating the detection substrate from low to high dimensions and constructing a hierarchical pore structure not only provides more detection sites to enhance its sensitivity but also effectively improve the mechanical properties of the aerogel for improve its recyclability in detection and adsorption (L. T. Zhu et al., 2018). Additionally, this strategy mitigates challenges associated with direct MOF application, including shedding, poor retention, and non-uniformity (Ma, Lou, Chen, Shi, & Xu, 2019).

Considering the above issues, this work is aimed at preparing a bifunctional platform of hierarchical pore NC@MOF aerogel microspheres (BMATXHY) for simultaneous fluorescence detection and removal of TC residues in water by in-situ growth of fluorescent Zr-MOF on bacterial nanocellulose (BC). The chemical composition, fluorescence properties, quenching, and adsorption mechanisms of BMATXHY were systematically investigated. The macropores and mesopores in the aerogel microspheres provide efficient channels for the transfer of target molecules, which significantly improves the mass transfer efficiency. The micropores and active sites in the highly stable Zr-MOF greatly enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of the detection and adsorption processes. Meanwhile, in-situ growth ensures the stability of detection and adsorption. The design of aerogel microspheres not only improves the processability of the material but also enhances the convenience and recyclability of detection and adsorption. This work creates a novel bifunctional platform for efficient fluorescence detection and adsorption of TC, which shows a promising application.

Zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4, >99 %), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, >99 %), 4-(4-carboxy-N-(4-carboxyphenyl) anilino) benzoic acid (H3TCA, >99 %), methanol (>99 %), tert-Butanol (TBA)(>99 %), Tetracycline (TC, >99 %), Sulfamethazine (SM2, >99 %), Ciprofloxacin (CIP, >99 %), Metronidazole (MDE, >99 %) and Erythromycin (EM,>99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Maclean's Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR) was purchased from Kunshan Jincheng Reagent Co., Ltd. Bacterial nanocellulose (BC) was purchased from Guilin Qihong Technology Co., Ltd.

BC were biosynthesized according to the method reported previously (Yang et al., 2009). Briefly, BC was first generated from acetobacter acetic in static culture. Subsequently, it was purified by 1 % NaOH, and washed by distilled water to neutral, then stirred and dispersed at high speed to obtained bacterial cellulose dispersion. Zeta potential of BC is −16.6 mV. As shown by the TEM image of BC (Fig. 1 ), the fiber diameter is in the range of 50–100 nm and the length is >20 μm. It has a large aspect ratio. For other characterisations of BC such as FTIR, XRD, TG, BET, etc., please refer to Section 3.1.

Firstly, the antiseptic in the BC suspension is washed by centrifugation with deionized water. Secondly, the TBA/H2O ratio is adjusted to acquire a BC suspension with a concentration of 0.5 wt%. Then, the BC suspension is dropped into liquid nitrogen using a syringe to rapidly freeze the water molecules and form water channels(R. M. Yu et al., 2017). Finally, BC microspheres with a three-dimensional porous structure and a density of 5 mg/cm3 were obtained by freeze-drying, named BATX (BATX, where X represents the ratio of TBA/H2O, TBA content = 0 wt%, 10 wt%, 30 wt%, 60 wt%, corresponding to X = 0, 1, 3, 6, respectively.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Zr-MOF and BC@MOF aerogel microspheres (BMATXHY)
ce:section.ce:para: ZrCl4 (86.3 mg) and H3TCA (94.2 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of DMF, and the solutions were mixed in a 50 mL hydrothermal synthesis reactor. HCl (8 mL, 36 wt%) was then added as the modulating agent. The system was reacted at 120 °C for 48 h to obtain Zr-MOF (Xu et al., 2022). Regarding the synthesis of BC@MOF aerogel microspheres, firstly, the BC microspheres (6 mg) are soaked in a solution containing ZrCl4 (60.9 mg) in DMF solution for 3 h. Secondly, DMF solution containing H3TCA (20 mg) is added and soaked for 1 h. Subsequently, the mixture is transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal synthesis reactor with varying levels of hydrochloric acid and sonicated for 10 min. Then, the reaction is carried out at 120 °C for 24 h to produce BC@MOF aerogel microspheres (BMATXHY, where X represents the ratio of TBA/H2O, TBA content = 0 wt%, 10 wt%, 30 wt%, 60 wt%, corresponding to X = 0, 1, 3, 6, respectively. Y represents the mass percentage of HCl in the whole system = 1 wt%, 5 wt%, corresponding to Y = 1, 5, respectively.). Finally, the synthesized composites are washed three times with DMF, methanol and deionized water.

Detection of TC by Zr-MOF: the concentration of Zr-MOF suspension was set as 0.025 mg/mL, and the concentration range of antibiotics were controlled to be in the range of 0–2.5 μM. Then the fluorescence spectra of the suspension were recorded.

Detection of TC by BMATXHY: BMATXHY was immersed in TC solution (different concentration and pH) or different antibiotics solution (SM2, CIP, and EM) for a certain period at room temperature, and then the fluorescence spectra of BMATXHY were measured. In practical engineering applications, water samples were taken and diluted 10-fold, 100-fold, and 1000-fold for comparison experiments to ensure the accuracy of detection. The details of the calculation methodology can be found in the Supporting Information.

A series of antibiotics solutions with different concentrations (TC, SM2, and CIP) were prepared, and the standard curves of different antibiotics were obtained by measuring the absorbance at the maximum absorption wavelength (Fig. S11–12). Adsorption kinetics was studied by collecting an appropriate volume of antibiotic solution at predetermined adsorption time intervals (C
0 = 10 mg/L, V = 50 mL, adsorbent dosage = 1 mg). The adsorption isotherms were studied by preparing a series of antibiotic solutions at different concentrations. The solutions were shaken with the adsorbent at a constant temperature of 25 °C for 12 h to ensure that adsorption equilibrium was reached (C
0 = 2–40 mg/L, V = 10 mL, adsorbent dosage = 0.1 mg). All adsorption experiments and measurements were repeated three times. The details of calculations, please refer to the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Hospital outfalls (Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences Dermatological Hospital, Nanjing), lake water from Xuanwu Lake Park (Nanjing), and tap water were collected. All water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μM microporous filter membrane. Different concentrations of TC standard solutions were added to the pretreated samples (Xing et al., 2024). Three measurements were taken in parallel for each set of samples to ensure the reliability of the probe in the natural water environment. Details of the calculations can be found in the Supporting Information.

To investigate the interaction mode and demonstrate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the sensor BMAT3H5 for antibiotics, the DMol3 module on Materials Studio (MS) software was carried out to simulate and optimize the molecules of the sensor BMAT3H5 and antibiotics. The structure optimization of the sensor BMAT3H5 and the antibiotic was first carried out using the DMol3 module, and the exchange-correlation generalization adopted was the LDA (PWC) method, with the electron spin set to spin-limiting and the numerical basis group (size) set to DND (3.5). The computational accuracy was medium throughout the calculations. After the structure optimization, the frontier molecular orbitals of the molecular (HOMO-LUMO) were calculated by selecting the “Energy” calculation task in the DMol3 module (Peng et al., 2020).

In order to study the nature of adsorption processes and interactions, the adsorption energies of different antibiotics on BMAT3H5 adsorbents were predicted using the adsorption localization module in MS. The antibiotic molecules and BMAT3H5 adsorbent structural units were first structurally optimized using the Forcite module prior to the calculations by selecting the COMPASSII force field. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were also used in this study to further understand the interaction between antibiotic molecules and BMAT3H5 (Khnifira et al., 2021).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained via a Regulus SU8100 instrument along with recording energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra. The FT-IR spectra were collected using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Electron Nicolet-360, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was determined by Rigaku Ultima IV using Cu Kα radiation. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a TA Instruments (TGA 209 F1, Netzsch). The ultraviolet-visible spectrum was measured using a spectrophotometer (V-1800, Mapada). The specific surface area of the composite was determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method on an analyzer (ASAP 2460, USA) at a liquid nitrogen temperature of 77 K. The fluorescence measurements were performed using a FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK), and the excitation wavelength was fixed at 365 nm with a slit width of 2 nm.

The bifunctional platform for the enrichment and detection of TC based on fluorescent microspheres with hierarchical pore structure was fabricated via in-situ growth of MOF in BC microspheres, as shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, BC microspheres were prepared in liquid nitrogen by adjusting the proportion of TBA in the BC solution. Subsequently, these BC microspheres were immersed in a solution containing the central metal ion (Zr4+). The BC rich in hydroxyl groups provides a large number of reactive sites and strong binding interactions, which facilitate the anchoring of Zr4+ to the BC through interactions such as electrostatic attraction and ligand reactions. In the presence of an appropriate ratio of the organic ligand H3TCA, the central metal ion reacts with the ligand, resulting in the in-situ growth of Zr-MOF with fluorescent properties and microporosity within the BC microspheres. The macropores and mesopores in the aerogel microspheres provide effective channels for the transfer of target molecules, which can improve the mass transfer efficiency, and the active micropores in the fluorescent Zr-MOF can improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the detection and adsorption process, resulting in the formation of BC@MOF aerogel microspheres (BMATXHY) for the adsorption and detection of TC.

In order to make the spatial network and pore structure of microspheres more homogeneous, they can be optimized by adjusting the TBA/H2O ratio. TBA is a low-polar organic solvent, which can homogeneously disperse nanocellulose and influences the crystallite size, thus improving the pore structure and specific surface area of nanocellulose microspheres through freeze drying (F. Jiang & Hsieh, 2014; Nemoto, Saito, & Isogai, 2015). BAT3 has the most homogeneous spatial network structure (Fig. 2a-c). Zr-MOF is in the form of nanoflowers with a diameter of approximately 1.5 nm (Fig. S1), indicating no effect of spatial confinement on the solvothermal synthesis (Xu et al., 2022). In addition, it is noted that the spatial steric hindrance limits the increase in the size of Zr-MOF crystals in-situ grown on BC and effectively prevented their aggregation, resulting in a significant increase in the specific surface area and accessibility.

As an acidic modulator, HCl can coordinate with metal ions and compete with the ligand to restrict the growth of MOF in specific directions, thereby generating more defects and active sites (Jiang et al., 2021). At the same time, HCl modulates the pH and ion concentration of the solution, conditions that also affect the growth rate and morphology of MOF. The crystalline morphology and fluorescence properties of BMATXHY were optimized by adjusting the amount of HCl. As shown in Fig. 2d-i, Zr-MOF nucleates and grows most uniformly on BC with more defects on the surface when the HCl content is 5 %. Elemental mapping further confirms the uniform distribution of Zr-MOF in the BAT3 (Fig. 2j-l).

The fluorescence properties of Zr-MOF and BMATXHY were evaluated under the optimum excitation wavelength of 345 nm. Fig. S3 clearly demonstrates the fluorescence changes of BMATXHY before and after the adsorption of TC. As shown in Fig. S4, the highest quenching efficiency of the complexes for TC fluorescence is achieved at the TBA /H2O ratio of 30 % and the HCl content of 5 %. It may be attributed to the uniformly dispersed 3D porous structure, which promotes the mass transfer of TC molecules. In addition, the presence of more defects and active sites within MOF layer also facilitate the interaction with TC. In particular, the fluorescence intensity of BMAT3H5 is approximately 1.5 times as high as that of Zr-MOF, due to the small size effect of in-situ grown crystals (Fig. S5) (Thanh, Maclean, & Mahiddine, 2014). Since BMAT3H5 exhibits the highest quenching efficiency towards TC, it is selected as the representative sample for evaluating structural features.

XRD confirms the crystal structures of Zr-MOF, BMAT3H5 and BAT3. As shown in Fig. 3a, BMAT3H5 and Zr-MOF exhibit the same diffraction peaks (100), (110), (130), and (401) (Xu et al., 2022). However, the intensity of the BMAT3H5 peak is reduced due to the increase in crystal defects, which is advantageous for providing active sites for adsorption and fluorescence detection (Y. Liu et al., 2022). In addition, the signal of BAT3 in BMAT3H5 is not obvious because the encapsulation layer of Zr-MOF outside the BC is too thick and obscures the signal of BAT3. The FTIR spectra of BMAT3H5 exhibit characteristic peaks of both BAT3 and Zr-MOF, verifying the complexation of Zr-MOF with BAT3 (Fig. S6). The results of TGA analysis of different samples are compared in Fig. 3b. BC@MOF has good thermal stability and remains stable before 500 °C, which is significantly higher than BAT3 at 325 °C.

The hierarchical pore structure of the aerogel microspheres is analyzed by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distribution plots. Both Zr-MOF, BMAT3H5, and BAT3 belong to the type IV curves (Ambroz, Macdonald, Martis, & Parkin, 2018), and the specific surfaces areas are calculated to be 503.6, 568.3 and 65.2 m2/g, respectively (Fig. 2c). The curve for Zr-MOF has a typical hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of mesoporous (Ambroz et al., 2018). For BMAT3H5, the curves increase dramatically when P/P
0 < 0.1, indicating the presence of a large number of micropores. Additionally, the hysteresis loop in the curve signifies the existence of mesopores. Due to the template effect and spatial site resistance of BC, the degree of crystal agglomeration is reduced, enabling better control of crystal size. The pore size distribution reveals that the BAT3 mainly contain mesopores and macropores with an average pore size of 29.4 nm (Fig. 3d). The Zr-MOF mainly contain mesopores with an average pore size of 10.5 nm. BMAT3H5 has a hierarchical pore structure with mainly 1.04 nm micropores, followed by 4.46 nm and 35.59 nm mesopores. The presence of macropores is evident from the SEM image (Fig. 2h). The BMAT3H5 is the typical hierarchical pore material.

Detection sensitivity is the most critical index of fluorescent probes. In this section, the effects of TBA and HCl usage on the fluorescence detection performance are investigated. Stern-Volmer plots of I
0
/I versus TC concentration for aerogel microspheres are shown in Fig. S7, and the corresponding emission spectra are illustrated in Fig. S8. Fig. 4a shows the CIE 1931 color diagram with TC added. The Zr-MOF has a K
SV of 2.55 ± 0.001 × 105 M−1 and an LOD of 211 ± 0.047 nM. By comparison, BMAT3H5 exhibits the most effective detection performance for TC (Fig. 4d). There is a high correlation coefficient for BMAT3H5 in the linear range of 0–2.5 μM (R
2 = 0.991). LOD and K
SV are calculated to be 1.89 ± 0.001 × 106 M−1 and 28 ± 0.012 nM, respectively (Fig. 4b-c). When compared to MOFs reported for TC detection in recent years, BMAT3H5 demonstrates superior quenching efficiency and detection sensitivity (Fig. 4e) (X. Wu et al., 2022) (Y. Q. Zhang, Wu, Mao, Tao, & Li, 2019) (Yazhini et al., 2022) (Y. Q. Zhang et al., 2019) (B. Zhu et al., 2020). The improved detection sensitivity of BMAT3H5 compared to Zr-MOF can be attributed to the smaller-sized and uniformly distributed Zr-MOF layers grown in-situ on the BC surface. These layers improve the accessibility and provide additional binding sites for target analytes. Furthermore, the hierarchical pore structure of the aerogel microspheres facilitates the mass transfer efficiency of the TC molecules, enabling rapid and sensitive detection. Meanwhile, the fluorescence performance of BC@MOF was much higher than that of CNF@MOF compared with other nanocelluloses.

The selectivity and anti-interference properties of the sensor are crucial factors that need to be taken into account in practical detection. To assess its selectivity, the fluorescence response of BMAT3H5 to four commonly used antibiotics was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4f, the fluorescence intensity of BMAT3H5 is nearly quenched by TC, whereas the other antibiotics maintain high fluorescence intensity, suggesting that BMAT3H5 can selectively recognize TC.

The pH serves as a crucial factor influencing the form of antibiotic ions in solution (Safavi-Mirmahalleh, Salami-Kalajahi, & Roghani-Mamaqani, 2020). The quenching efficiency of TC towards BMAT3H5 maintains good stability across the pH range of 3–9 (Fig. 4g), achieving the peak at pH 7 (92.10 %). To investigate the long-term stability of BMAT3H5, its fluorescence intensity was continuously monitored when stored at room temperature. As depicted in Fig. 4h, there is no notable change in the fluorescence intensity of BMAT3H5 over a period of 1 to 30 days. Additionally, the XRD patterns of BMAT3H5 immersed in water, NaOH, and HCl solutions for 7 days were investigated (Fig. 4i). BMAT3H5 retains its original crystal structure in neutral, acidic, and alkaline environments. This indicates that BMAT3H5 possesses excellent stability and durability for practical applications in various environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanisms of BMAT3H5 for TC
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the fluorescence quenching of BMAT3H5 by TC, mechanisms including structural conversion, chemical conversion, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and internal filtration effect (IFE) are investigated.

FTIR reveals the interaction between BMAT3H5 and TC (Fig. 5a). After TC detection, the absorption bands of OH or NH (3334 cm−1), -NH2 (1056 cm−1), and the benzene ring (1579, 1387, 778, and 661 cm−1) in BMAT3H5 shift to higher wavenumbers, indicating that these groups are involved in the detection process (R. Zhao et al., 2020). XPS is used to further investigate the interaction between TC and BMAT3H5 (Fig. 5b). The peak with binding energy at 182.8 and 185.2 eV in the high-resolution spectra is assigned to Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 5c). In the N 1s spectra (Fig. 5d), the CN bond is shifted from 400.4 to 400.2 eV, and in the C 1s spectra (Fig. 5e), the CC content decreases and the CO content increases after adsorption. These changes suggest that π-π stacking leads to a decrease in the electron density of the BMAT3H5, which induces the transfer of electrons from the -COOH of the BMAT3H5 to the -NH2 of the TC (Zhong, Yuan, Xiong, Wu, & Lou, 2023). The electrostatic interaction between BMAT3H5 and TC was further demonstrated by characterizing the zeta potential. When BMAT3H5 came into contact with TC, charge transfer or charge rearrangement occurred between them, resulting in a change in the zeta potential of BMAT3H5 (Fig. S9). FTIR, XPS profiles, and zeta potential indicate strong interactions between TC and BMAT3H5, including hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking.

Static and dynamic quenching are the two primary forms of luminescence quenching. XRD patterns show that the main diffraction peaks of BMAT3H5 remain unchanged throughout the quenching process, demonstrating that no new complex is formed between BMAT3H5 and TC (Fig. S10). Moreover, this verifies that the fluorescence quenching of BMAT3H5 in the presence of TC belongs to the dynamic burst (Deng et al., 2024). UV–Vis absorption spectra have been used to explore the mechanism of competitive absorption of irradiated light (Fig. 4f) (Zhai et al., 2023). The apparent overlap between the excitation spectra of BMAT3H5 and the UV–Vis absorption spectra of TC confirms the existence of an inner filter effect (IFE) between TC and BMAT3H5 (Li et al., 2023). When compared to other antibiotics, TC covers the excitation bands of BMAT3H5 to a significant extent, resulting in the highest quenching efficiency. Meanwhile, the emission spectrum of BMAT3H5 hardly overlaps with the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of TC, so the FRET is excluded (S. Jiang et al., 2024; Q. Liu et al., 2019).

Based on the PET theory, we calculated the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) energy levels and the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) energy levels of the BMAT3H5 and antibiotics using MS (Fig. 6 ). The HOMO-LUMO energy level difference (ΔE) is an important factor influencing on electron transfer, and a low ΔE favors electron transfer between the sensor and the analyte (Murugan, Prakash, Jayakumar, Sundaramurthy, & Sundramoorthy, 2019). When BMAT3H5 is excited by light and absorbs the energy of the photon, the electron on the HOMO with higher energy can jump to the LUMO with lower energy. Because the HOMO of TC is located between the HOMO and LUMO of the fluorescent molecule, the electrons on the HOMO of TC with electron donating ability will be transferred to the HOMO of BMAT3H5 (Y. S. Wang, Yu, Zhang, Jia, & Ji, 2021). The electron transfer caused that the excited electrons on the LUMO cannot return to the original HOMO, and then the fluorescence of BMAT3H5 is quenched (S. Zhao et al., 2022).

The above studies indicate that the PET, IFE, and the strong interaction between BMAT3H5 and TC are the main factors contributing to the selective quenching of TC (Lai, Jin, Shi, Zhou, & Li, 2023; Ren et al., 2023; Webber, 2008).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of BMAT3H5 for TC
ce:section.ce:para:
As a bifunctional platform, the BMAT3H5 not only demonstrates high sensitivity in fluorescence detection of TC at extremely low concentrations but also exhibits outstanding adsorption performance at high concentrations. The kinetic data between BMAT3H5 and TC have been analyzed using pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models (Table S1). Adsorption of TC by BMAT3H5 reached equilibrated in only 5 min (Fig. 7a). This is due to the hierarchical pore structure of BMAT3H5 which provides efficient and rapid pathways for the mass transfer of TC, and the large number of defects and active sites in BMAs also facilitates the interaction with TC. It is noted that the pseudo-second-order kinetic model is more effective in simulating the adsorption of TC，which is primarily controlled by chemisorption process (J. L. Wang & Guo, 2020b). The adsorption capacity was further analyzed using the Langmuir and Freundlich models (Table S2) (J. L. Wang & Guo, 2020a). As depicted in Fig. 7b, the Langmuir model is more suitable for describing the adsorption process of TC on BMAT3H5. These results reveal that the adsorption of TC on BMAT3H5 follows a uniform monolayer adsorption pattern, with the maximum adsorption capacity of 317.6 mg/g (Amrhar, El Gana, & Mobarak, 2021). Meanwhile, we explored the effect of diameter size on the adsorption performance of BMAT3H5. As shown in Fig. S13, the adsorption rate of microspheres with large diameters slightly decreased but did not affect the adsorption amount. In addition, the effect of BAT3 (BC) on TC detection and adsorption was explored. As shown in Fig. S14, the Q
m of BAT3 was only 48 mg/g, which was much lower than that of BMAT3H5, and BAT3 did not fluoresce.

Fig. 7c. illustrates the maximum adsorption of BMAT3H5 on different antibiotics. It is noted that the adsorption of BMAT3H5 for TC remarkably exceeds those of the other three antibiotics. The adsorption selectivity to TC is consistent with the difference in the fluorescence quenching efficiencies of various antibiotics (Fig. 4f).

The adsorption energy (E
ads ) serves as a crucial parameter for evaluating the adsorption capacity of antibiotics. To gain a deeper understanding of the mechanism of selective adsorption of TC by BMAT3H5, E
ads of various antibiotics on BMAT3H5 are calculated by MS (Khnifira et al., 2021). The conformation of the constructed structure was optimized from both geometrical and energetic perspectives (Fig. 8 ). Fig. 7d presents the E
ads between antibiotics and adsorbents. Notably, the E
ads of the BMAT3H5-water-TC structure is lower than that of other antibiotic-adsorbent combinations, suggesting a more stable adsorption conformation (Gholami, Shokuhi Rad, Heydarinasab, & Ardjmand, 2016). Among the four antibiotics, TC exhibits the lowest E
ads (−331.13 Kcal/mol) and the highest Q
m (317.6 mg/g). The theoretical calculations align closely with the actual experimental results, further confirming the high adsorption capacity and selectivity of BMAT3H5 for TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti interference performance and recycling performance of BMAT3H5
ce:section.ce:para: The immunity of the sensor to interference is crucial for accurate detection in actual water environments. Twelve common ions (5-fold excess: Cu2+, Ba2+, H2PO4 −, Cl−, I−, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ni+, Co2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, and NO3 −) were selected as competing ions to simulate a real aqueous environment when fluorescent detection TC using BMAT3H5. As shown in Fig. 9a, the majority of these ions do not impact the fluorescence of BMAT3H5 or interfere with TC detection. In addition, we selected a variety of antibiotics that are representative of both environmental and clinical applications as potential interferences (5-fold excess: SM2, CIP, EM, MDE) to complement the competition detection experiments of BMAT3H5 on TC. As shown in Fig. S15, the coexistence of these antibiotics hardly affected the fluorescence detection of TC by BMAT3H5. In a word, BMAT3H5 exhibits excellent anti-interference capabilities in fluorescent detection of TC.

The reusability of sensors and adsorbents is essential for cost-effective monitoring of ecological pollution levels in large scale aqueous environments. Compared with powdered Zr-MOF, BMAT3H5 has better hydrodynamic properties for better manipulation and recovery in practical applications. In the recovery experiments, five regeneration cycles were performed using methanol as desorbent, and three experiments were conducted to obtain accurate average values. Fig. 9b-c clearly shows the excellent optical stability and reversibility of BMAT3H5 as a sensor, as well as its periodic fluorescence “off-on” response to TC. Fig. 9d demonstrates the excellent adsorption-desorption performance of BMAT3H5 as an adsorbent. Fig. 10 shows the mechanism of sensitive detection and efficient adsorption of TC by the BMAT3H5 bifunctional platform. Compared to Zr-MOF, the composite aerogel microspheres exhibit superior water and pH stability along with enhanced fluorescence properties. The hierarchical pore structure enhances the detection sensitivity and adsorption capacity of the composites. In addition, high selectivity and recyclability provide a good basis for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the usefulness and reliability of BMAT3H5, TC is detected in Aquaculture area, lake water, hospital outfall, and tap water. Detection accuracy was analyzed using spiked recovery experiments as shown in Table 1 (Chen et al., 2025). The recoveries of TC obtained through fluorescence determination range from 95.8 % to 101.7 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) <4.1 %. Furthermore, the results from the fluorescence sensor align well with those obtained using the conventional UV–vis, indicating that BMAT3H5 has good tractability for TC quantification in real samples.

In this work, a bifunctional platform for simultaneous detection and removal of TC was successfully constructed by in-situ growth of Zr-MOF in BC microspheres. Compared with single Zr-MOF, BMAT3H5 exhibits superior water and pH stability along with enhanced fluorescence detection properties. Its unique hierarchical pore structure not only achieved an adsorption capacity of up to 317.6 mg/g, but also significantly enhanced the interaction between TC and Zr-MOF through the enrichment effect, which drastically improved the TC detection sensitivity. As expected, BMAT3H5 has a LOD as low as 28 ± 0.012 nM and a K
SV as high as 1.89 ± 0.001 × 106 M−1. This performance outperformed most of the results of similar studies in recent years, demonstrating excellent TC detection and capability. Meanwhile, in real sample testing, BMAT3H5 also performed well, validating its reliability and effectiveness in complex environments. The good selectivity to TC was theoretically validated through simulations with Materials Studio software. In addition, the design of aerogel microspheres not only improves the processability of the material, but also broadens its application scenarios, facilitates the recycling of the detection and adsorption process, reduces the operation cost, and improves the environmental friendliness. It provides a novel and practical bifunctional platform for efficient fluorescence detection and adsorption of TC, which has a broad application prospect in the fields of environmental monitoring, water treatment, and food safety testing.
